Name: Verify that the endpoint successfully generates a frontend JWT token when a valid empty JSON body is provided.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json and an empty JSON body {}. 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 200 OK. 3. Verify the response body contains a non-empty string representing the JWT token. 4. Validate the structure of the returned token (e.g., three parts separated by dots).
Description: Verify that the endpoint successfully generates a frontend JWT token when a valid empty JSON body is provided.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 200 OK. Response body contains a valid JWT token string (e.g., 'eyJ... .eyJ... . ...').

Name: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests with a malformed JSON body, preventing parsing errors or unexpected behavior.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json and a malformed JSON body (e.g., '{invalid json'). 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 400 Bad Request. 3. Verify the error message is generic and does not expose internal server details.
Description: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests with a malformed JSON body, preventing parsing errors or unexpected behavior.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 400 Bad Request. Response body contains a generic error message indicating a malformed request body (e.g., 'Invalid JSON format' or 'Bad Request').

Name: Verify that the endpoint only accepts POST requests and rejects other HTTP methods, preventing unintended operations.
Steps: 1. Send a GET request to /api/auth/frontend-token. 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 405 Method Not Allowed. 3. Verify the response body contains a generic error message.
Description: Verify that the endpoint only accepts POST requests and rejects other HTTP methods, preventing unintended operations.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 405 Method Not Allowed. Response body contains a generic error message (e.g., 'Method Not Allowed').

Name: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests with an unsupported Content-Type header, ensuring proper data handling and preventing unexpected input types.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: text/plain and an empty body. 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 415 Unsupported Media Type or 400 Bad Request. 3. Verify the error message is generic.
Description: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests with an unsupported Content-Type header, ensuring proper data handling and preventing unexpected input types.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 415 Unsupported Media Type or 400 Bad Request. Response body contains a generic error message (e.g., 'Unsupported Media Type' or 'Bad Request').

Name: Verify that the endpoint handles requests with unexpected extra fields in the JSON body gracefully, preventing potential injection or misinterpretation of data.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json and a body like '{"unexpected_field": "value", "another_field": 123}'. 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 400 Bad Request. 3. Verify the error message is generic and indicates invalid input.
Description: Verify that the endpoint handles requests with unexpected extra fields in the JSON body gracefully, preventing potential injection or misinterpretation of data.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 400 Bad Request. Response body contains a generic error message indicating invalid or unexpected fields (e.g., 'Unexpected fields in request body' or 'Bad Request').

Name: Verify that the generated JWT token adheres to the expected format (header.payload.signature) and contains a valid future expiry timestamp (exp claim).
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json and an empty JSON body {}. 2. Extract the JWT token from the 200 OK response. 3. Decode the token (e.g., using a JWT library) and verify it has three parts separated by dots. 4. Verify the presence of 'exp' (expiry) and 'iat' (issued at) claims in the payload. 5. Verify that 'exp' is a future timestamp relative to 'iat' and the current time, and within a reasonable duration (e.g., 15-30 minutes).
Description: Verify that the generated JWT token adheres to the expected format (header.payload.signature) and contains a valid future expiry timestamp (exp claim).
Precondition: API service is running and accessible. A successful token has been generated (e.g., from APIMSDAT-001).
Expected result: The returned token is a valid JWT string. The decoded token payload contains 'exp' and 'iat' claims, with 'exp' being a future timestamp relative to 'iat' and the current time.

Name: Verify that the endpoint implements rate limiting to prevent Denial of Service (DoS) attacks through rapid, repeated token generation requests.
Steps: 1. Send a burst of N (e.g., 100) POST requests to /api/auth/frontend-token within a short timeframe (e.g., 5 seconds) from the same client/IP. 2. Observe the HTTP status codes for these requests. 3. Verify that after a certain number of successful requests, subsequent requests return HTTP status 429 Too Many Requests. 4. Verify that the response for 429 status contains appropriate headers (e.g., Retry-After, X-RateLimit-Limit, X-RateLimit-Remaining, X-RateLimit-Reset).
Description: Verify that the endpoint implements rate limiting to prevent Denial of Service (DoS) attacks through rapid, repeated token generation requests.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible. Rate limiting mechanism is configured on the server.
Expected result: Initial requests return 200 OK. After exceeding the rate limit, subsequent requests return 429 Too Many Requests with relevant rate limit headers.

Name: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests sent without a request body, as an empty JSON object is explicitly expected.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json but without any request body. 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 400 Bad Request. 3. Verify the error message is generic and indicates a missing or invalid body.
Description: Verify that the endpoint rejects requests sent without a request body, as an empty JSON object is explicitly expected.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 400 Bad Request. Response body contains a generic error message indicating a missing request body (e.g., 'Request body is missing' or 'Bad Request').

Name: Verify that the endpoint correctly processes the request and generates a token even if an 'X-Frontend-JWT' header is present, as it is not required for this endpoint, ensuring robustness.
Steps: 1. Send a POST request to /api/auth/frontend-token with Content-Type: application/json, an empty JSON body {}, and an 'X-Frontend-JWT' header with a dummy or invalid token value (e.g., 'invalid.token.string'). 2. Verify the HTTP status code is 200 OK. 3. Verify the response body contains a new, valid JWT token. The presence of the unnecessary header should not interfere with token generation.
Description: Verify that the endpoint correctly processes the request and generates a token even if an 'X-Frontend-JWT' header is present, as it is not required for this endpoint, ensuring robustness.
Precondition: API service is running and accessible.
Expected result: HTTP status 200 OK. Response body contains a valid, newly generated JWT token. The unnecessary header should be ignored.
